summary: Patrick McKenzie and Keith Perhac discuss strategies for transitioning from consulting to product-based revenue streams. Patrick explains why he wound down his consulting business after a Fortune 500 engagement collapsed and took months of his pipeline with it, highlighting the fragility of consulting income. They cover multiple productization models: retainer agreements with existing clients, licensing pre-written code, creating info-products (courses and books) from consulting expertise, and building products for other companies' platforms. The conversation emphasizes that retainer agreements would have been 'transformatively good' for Patrick's consulting business, and that even 90% of paid info-product content may be freely available but the curation and packaging create enormous value.
key_points: Retainer agreements transform feast-or-famine consulting into predictable recurring revenue by charging clients a fraction of your weekly rate monthly to maintain and iterate on delivered engagements
Info-products aren't cannibalizing consulting — they're gateway drugs that demonstrate expertise and drive higher-value consulting engagements
Building products for other companies' platforms (Twilio, AWeber, Infusionsoft) gives you access to their audience, as platform companies are incentivized to promote anything that drives adoption
Tiered pricing (1X, 2.2X, 5X) with increasing levels of access and guaranteed outcomes captures value from both price-sensitive and premium buyers
Having 300 customers instead of 3 clients means you no longer have bosses — you have people whose business you can take or leave
money_quotes: The first way to never get screwed about a $10,000 check is to not really need it.
If you are a W-2 employee, you have one boss. If you move into consulting and have three clients, who are each responsible for about one third of your billing, you now have three bosses.
Consulting clients really want to buy dramatic blog post readings as a service.
People who don't have employees don't understand how expensive employees are.
As an expert, you don't know what other people don't know.
They're going to be writing a $10,000 payroll check with the memo 'Reading free information on the Internet.'
Retainer agreements would have been absolutely transformatively good for my consulting business.
You don't phrase things as in, 'I don't have time,' because you always have time. You say, 'It is not a priority.'
key_insight: Consultants should systematically convert one-time engagements into recurring revenue through retainer agreements, licensed tools, and info-products extracted from their existing expertise, because consulting without residual income is inherently fragile.
